Y BooleanNetworks (BNs) are discrete-time (autonomous)
S dynamical systems whose describing variables are con-
.
s strained to take values in the Boolean set. When their
s evolution is controlled by an external signal (an input),
e
whose entries  in turn take Boolean values,  they are
e
[ called Boolean Control Networks (BCNs). The interest
 
  in this class of logical systems originated in the 1960s,
1 when Stuart Kauffman introduced them to model and
v analyze gene regulatory networks Kauffman (1969), and
7
even if this may still be regarded as their most success-
6
ful application area, they have been adopted in a wide
6
range of fields,  including systems biology,  computer
1
science, game theory, smart cities and network theory
1
. (see, e.g., Bornholdt (2008), Cheng (2014), Green et al.
5 (2007), Kabir et al. (2014)).
0
The algebraic approach to BNs and BCNs, proposed by
5
Cheng et al. Cheng (2009), Cheng &Qi (2010a,b), Cheng
2
: et al. (2011), and deeply relying on the concept of semi-
v tensor product (STP) Cheng (2001), hasmade it possible
i
to represent BNs and BCNs by means of discrete-time
X
state space models in which the state, input and out-
r
a put are logical vectors, and the describing matrices are
logical matrices, in turn. In this way, concepts and tools
developed for linear time-invariant state space models
have been adjusted to formalize and solve control prob-
lems for BNs and BCNs (see, e.g., Fornasini & Valcher
(2014), Li et al. (2013)).

tà di Padova, via Gradenigo 6B, 35131 Padova, Italy
gly pervasive across all areas of control engineering. However,
etworks (BCNs) are still very limited. In this paper we aim
damental control problems, i.e., state feedback stabilization,
a limited amount of data generated by the network, without
fe control; output regulation.
Despite  the  simplifying  “on/off"  (“active/inactive",
“high/low") logic at the basis of BCNs, the size and
complexity of the physical systems they model (e.g.,
gene regulatory networks) make it difficult to derive an
accurate model of the network, as well as to obtain it by
means of identification techniques. On the other hand,
in recent times direct data-driven methods have gained
interest in the control community, given their attractive
feature of bypassing the identification phase, to directly
design the problem solution based on raw data. Yet, the
use of partial data to solve control problems for logical
networks is still at an early stage, and to the best of our
knowledge the first and only attempt in this direction
has been made in Leifeld et al. (2018), where a one-step
data-driven approach to solve the output stabilization
problem is proposed. More specifically, the authors in
Leifeld et al. (2018) first reconstruct from the available
data the output prediction matrix that maps the cur-
rent input/output pair to the set of all possible next
outputs, and then, based on this map, design an output
feedback law to stabilize the output of the BCN to a
desired value. However, in general, the set of all possible
next outputs contains more than one element and hence
the output prediction is possibly non deterministic.
This uncertainty is handled by resorting to Probabilistic
Boolean Networks (PBNs) to compensate for the fact
that data are not sufficient to identify the whole BCN
and uniquely solve the stabilization problem.
In this paper, we adopt a different viewpoint and exploit
the concept of informativity introduced by van Waarde,
Eising, and coauthors in van Waarde et al. (2020) (see,
also, Waarde et al. (2023)), to address the limitations
arising from having only a limited amount of data avail-
20 May 2025

A Boolean control network (BCN) is a logical system
described by the following equations
X(t + 1) = f(X(t), U(t)),  t ∈ Z+,  (1a)
Y (t) =h(X(t)),  (1b)
n
where X(t) ∈ B  is the n-dimensional state variable,
m  p
U(t) ∈ B  is the m-dimensional input, and Y (t) ∈ B  is
the p-dimensional output at time t. f and h are logical
n  m  n  n  p
functions, i.e., f : B ×B → B , while h : B → B . If
the logical system is autonomous, namely no input acts
on it, then the BCN becomes a Boolean network (BN)
and equation (1a) becomes
X(t + 1) = f(X(t)),  t ∈ Z+,  (2)
n  n
where f : B → B  is a logical function. By exploiting
the bijective correspondence between Boolean and logi-
cal vectors, the BN (2) can be equivalently expressed via
its algebraic representation Cheng et al. (2011) as
x(t + 1) = Lx(t),  t ∈ Z+,  (3)

Obviously, the matrix L of the BCN that has generated
the data belongs to Bd. However, Bd may also contain
other matrices if the data do not allow to uniquely de-
termine L. To this end, we introduce the notion of in-
formativity for identifiability.
Definition 5 We say that the data (xd, ud) are infor-
mative for identifiability of the BCN (4a) if Bd ≡ {L}.
We can provide the following characterization of infor-
mativity for identifiability.
Lemma 6 The data (xd, ud) are informative for identi-
fiability of the BCN (4a) if and only if the logical matrix
(cid:104)  (cid:105)
col1(Up) ⋉ col1(Xp) . . .  colT (Up) ⋉ colT (Xp)  (13)
is of full row rank.
Proof. A BCN (4a) is uniquely identified from the data
matrices Xp, Xf and Up if and only if these latter al-
low to deduce each column of the matrix L in (4a), or,
equivalently, are representative of every possible tran-
sition that can occur in the network (from all possible
state vectors and corresponding to all possible inputs).
By taking into account that the k-th column of the ma-
trix Xf represents the successor state of the state given
by the k-th column of Xp, when the applied input is the
one corresponding to the k-th column of Up, it follows
that we know from data every possible transition of the
BCN if and only if for every (i, j) ∈ [1,M]×[1, N] there
exists k ∈ [1, T ] such that

To make the following analysis meaningful, from now on
we assume that the collected data are not informative for
identifiability of the considered BCN, namely Bd ⊋ {L},
and we investigate under what conditions it is still pos-
sible to use them to solve some control problems.
We now formalize the data-driven state feedback stabi-
lization problem.
Problem 1 Given the data (xd, ud), determine (if it
exists) a state feedback matrix K ∈ LM×N such that
u(t) = Kx(t) stabilizes all the BCNs compatible with the
data to some state xe ∈ LN.
4

When Problem 1 is solvable, we will say that the data
(xd, ud) are informative for stabilization to xe ∈ LN by
state feedback.
ByProposition 4, it is possible to stabilize aBCN to some
state xe ∈ LN only if such a state is an equilibrium point
of the BCN. Therefore Problem 1 is solvable only if xe
is an equilibrium point for all possible BCNs compatible
with the data, namely all the BCNs in Bd. By Definition
2, a state xe is an equilibriumpoint of a BCN (4a) if there
i
exists i ∈ [1,M] such that for u = δM the successor of xe
is xe itself. Therefore, in order to identify the equilibrium
points compatible with the collected data, we can stack
the matrices Xp and Xf one above the other, and check
inwhich columns the logical vector in the upper matrix is
the same as the logical vector in the lower matrix. Such a
logical vector is an equilibriumpoint, since the column of
Xf represents the successor of the corresponding column
in Xp (for some input). More formally, we can apply the
following algorithm.

In Fornasini & Valcher (2016) the safe control of a BCN
was first addressed, then it was extended to handle ran-
dom impulsive logical control networks in Zhou et al.
(2023) and probabilistic Boolean control networks using
an event-triggered approach in Shao et al. (2025). The
2
This imposes T ≥ N.

safe control problem is stated as follows. Given a BCN
(4a), suppose that the set of states LN can be parti-
tioned into a set of unsafe states Xu and a set of safe
states Xs ≜ LN \Xu. Under what conditions is it possible
to design a control input so that every state trajectory
that originates from a safe state remains indefinitely in
Xs and every trajectory that starts from an unsafe state
enters Xs in a finite number of steps and there remains?
In Fornasini & Valcher (2016) it has been provided a
complete characterization of the problem solvability.
Proposition 12 Given a BCN (4a) and the set Xu of
unsafe states, the safe control problem is solvable if and
only if
(i) for every x ∈ Xs = LN \ Xu there exists u ∈ LM
such that L ⋉ u ⋉ x ∈ Xs;
(ii) the set Xs is reachable from every x ∈ Xu, which
amounts to saying that for every x ∈ Xu there exists
x¯ ∈ Xs such that x¯ is reachable from x.
Moreover, if the safe control problem is solvable, then it
is solvable by means of a state feedback law.
The data-driven version of the safe control problem is
the following one.
Problem 2 Given the data (xd, ud) and the unsafe set
Xu ⊊ LN, determine (if it exists) a state feedback con-
trol law u(t) = Kx(t), K ∈ LM×N, that solves the safe
control problem for every BCN compatible with the data.
When Problem 2 is solvable, the data (xd, ud) will be
called informative for safe control (with respect to the
unsafe set Xu). The problem solution relies on the tools
developed to solve Problem 1. First of all, we need to
identify from data if each state in Xs has (at least) one
successor in Xs. This amounts to verifying if for every
j  ℓ

Theorem 13 Problem 2  is  solvable  (i.e.,  the data
(xd, ud) are informative for safe control with respect
to the unsafe set Xu) if and only if the following two
conditions hold:

matrix K ∈ LM×N is given by  colr(K) = ur, ∀r ∈

Proof. (Necessity). Assume, by contradiction, that con-
dition (i) does not hold. This implies that there is at least
one state in Xs whose immediate successors, as they can
be deduced from data, are all in Xu. But this means that
there exists at least one BCN in Bd for which condition
(i) in Proposition 12 does not hold. For that BCN the
safe control problem is not solvable.
Assume now that (i) holds, but (ii) does not, namely

data on a finite time interval [0, T ], T ∈ Z+. However,
this time we collect also output trajectories. Let yd ≜

We extend the concept of compatibility of a BCN with
the collected data to the case in which the BCN is de-
scribed by (4), i.e., we include the output equation, and
the collected data are (xd, ud, yd). The set of Boolean
control networks (4) compatible with (xd, ud, yd) is

Obviously, the pair (L, H) corresponding to the BCN
o
(4) that generated the data belongs to Bd. Accordingly,
the notion of informativity for identifiability has to be
extended.
Definition 15 The data (xd, ud, yd) are called informa-
o
tive for identifiability of the BCN (4) if Bd ≡ {(L, H)}.
Even if the definition is different, the characterization
of the new notion of informativity for identifiability is
still the one in Lemma 6: the matrix in (13) must be of
full row rank. Necessity is obvious. On the other hand,
if the matrix in (13) is of full row rank, then we deduce
from the collected data every possible state transition
that can occur in the network. But this implies that all
possible states appear at least once in Xp (and conse-
quently also the corresponding output in Yp). This al-
lows to uniquely identify also the matrix H in (4b), and
hence the data (xd, ud, yd) are informative for identifia-
bility of the whole BCN (4).
In the sequel, we assume again that the data are not
informative for the identifiability of the BCN.
Problem 3 Given the data (xd, ud, yd), and some de-
∗
sired value y ∈ LP for the output in (4b), determine (if
possible) a state feedback matrix K ∈ LM×N such that,
by applying u(t) = Kx(t), for every initial condition
x(0), the output trajectories of all the BCNs compatible
∗  Z
with the data satisfy y(t) = y  for every t ≥ τ, ∃τ ∈ +.

When Problem 3 is solvable, we will say that the data
∗
(xd, ud, yd) are informative for output regulation to y ∈
LP by state feedback. In order to solve this problem, we
introduce some definitions and results, taken from the
model-based literature on BNs and BCNs (see, e.g., For-
nasini & Valcher (2013a, 2014)).
Definition 16 Given a BCN (4), the output regulation
∗
problem to y ∈ LP by state feedback is said to be solv-
able if there exist K ∈ LM×N and τ ∈ Z+ such that for
∗
every x(0) ∈ LN, y(t) = y , ∀t ≥ τ , when u(t) = Kx(t).
The first step of the model-based solution to this prob-
lem consists in finding all the states that generate the
∗
desired output y , namely the states belonging to the

lem by state feedback in a model-based set-up.

However, the next algorithm is able to also identify equi-
librium points, being them a special case of limit cycles.

The proposed algorithm is tailored for a sequence of data
collected in a single experiment. If the performed experi-
ments are more than one, it must be applied separately to

In this paper we have investigated how the recently in-
troduced data informativity approach van Waarde et al.
(2020) can be adapted and extended to solve fundamen-
tal control problems in Boolean control networks. More
specifically, we focused on the feedback stabilization, safe
control and output regulation problems, andwe provided
necessary and sufficient conditions, based only on previ-
ously collected data (supposed not to be informative for
identification), for their solvability. Moreover, we also
obtained from data the feedback matrices expressions.
We validated our results by means of examples.
